coredata.dc:title: Li-ion battery swelling force: Multiphysics coupling modeling and in-situ quantification for safety enhancement
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Quanqing
Fan, Liubin
Deng, Huanyong
Fang, Donglin
Wang, Can
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries typically exist in modular configurations, where individual cells are subjected to mechanical constraints upon assembled into groups. During charging, volume expansion of lithium-ion batteries induces substantial internal pressure within the battery pack, a phenomenon whose pressure evolution may compromise battery performance and even safety. To effectively manage the mechanical pressure on batteries, precise identification and quantification of the swelling force generated by expansion effects are critical. This study develops a detailed three-dimensional electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupled model, incorporating the actual layered structure of batteries. The model enable not only the visualization of stress distribution and deformation patterns across battery layers, but also the quantitative characterization of macroscopic swelling force dynamics. Its accuracy and reliability are rigorously validated against experimental data. Based on the model, reveal that the turning point of swelling force coincides with the moment when the lithiation rate at position P3 increases significantly compared with other positions. Moreover, due to the difference in Young's modulus between cathode and anode active materials, the cathode exhibits maximum stress but minimal strain at the end of charging, whereas the anode shows the opposite trend. This work offers guidance for both battery structural optimization and module assembly design, thereby contributing to improved safety of lithium-ion batteries during operation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Swelling force
Electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupling
Three-dimensional model
Battery safety

Validation and analysis of the thermal model
Validation and analysis of the mechanical model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overall experimental platform. (a) Measurement of battery dimensions. (b) Swelling force testing platform. (c) Battery system charge-discharge testing platform.

The parameter curves required for three physical locations. (a) The open-circuit potentials of the anode and cathode. (b) The entropic heat coefficients of anode and cathode materials. (c)–(d) The volumetric change rate of the active materials.
Grid independence verification. (a) The element counts of the three grid types. (b)–(d) Simulation comparisons of voltage, swelling force, and temperature under the three grid types.
Variations of swelling force, voltage, and temperature over time during charging under different preload forces and C-rates.

Differentials of swelling force, capacity, and voltage during charging under different preload forces and C-rates. (a) Differential of swelling force with respect to voltage. (b) Differential of swelling force with respect to capacity. (c) Differential of capacity with respect to voltage. (d) Differential of voltage with respect to capacity.

Electrical characteristics simulation during charging process. Comparison between simulation and experiment of battery terminal voltage under different preload forces: (a) Under 50 N. (b) Under 250 N. Evolution of potential distribution in the aluminum collector under different preload forces and rates: (c)–(f).

Temperature characteristics simulation during charging process. Comparison between simulation and experiment of battery side temperature under different preload forces: (a) Under 50 N. (b) Under 250 N. Evolution of temperature distribution on the battery surface under different preload forces and rates: (c)–(f).

Force characteristics simulation during charging process. Comparison between simulation and experiment of swelling force: (a) Under 50 N. (b) Under 250 N. Evolution of pressure distribution on the battery surface: (c)–(f).

Strain and stress distribution of battery components at end of charging. (a) Stress distribution of each battery component and local amplification of stress in a pair of electrodes. (b) Strain distribution of each battery component and local amplification of strain in a pair of electrodes.

Analysis of lithium-ion concentration in the anode. (a) Variations of lithium intercalation concentration with time. (b) Variations of lithiation level with time at three characteristic locations. Evolution of lithium intercalation concentration distribution in the anode region under different C-rates: (c) 1C. (d) 2C.
The detailed specific information of investigated pouch battery.

list-item.label: a)
list-item.para: Constant current discharge at 0.05C to the lower cutoff voltage

list-item.label: c)
list-item.para: Tighten the nut and adjust the preload to the target value

list-item.label: d)
list-item.para: Let stand for 2 h until force and temperature stabilize

list-item.label: e)
list-item.para: Perform charging tests according to the target C-rate

list-item.label: f)
list-item.para: Repeat steps a to f until all experiments are completed

head.ce:title: Li-ion battery swelling force: Multiphysics coupling modeling and in-situ quantification for safety enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automotive Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Weihai, Shandong 264209, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Automotive Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Weihai, Shandong 264209, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechatronics Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechatronics Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author.School of Mechatronics Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries typically exist in modular configurations, where individual cells are subjected to mechanical constraints upon assembled into groups. During charging, volume expansion of lithium-ion batteries induces substantial internal pressure within the battery pack, a phenomenon whose pressure evolution may compromise battery performance and even safety. To effectively manage the mechanical pressure on batteries, precise identification and quantification of the swelling force generated by expansion effects are critical. This study develops a detailed three-dimensional electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupled model, incorporating the actual layered structure of batteries. The model enable not only the visualization of stress distribution and deformation patterns across battery layers, but also the quantitative characterization of macroscopic swelling force dynamics. Its accuracy and reliability are rigorously validated against experimental data. Based on the model, reveal that the turning point of swelling force coincides with the moment when the lithiation rate at position P3 increases significantly compared with other positions. Moreover, due to the difference in Young's modulus between cathode and anode active materials, the cathode exhibits maximum stress but minimal strain at the end of charging, whereas the anode shows the opposite trend. This work offers guidance for both battery structural optimization and module assembly design, thereby contributing to improved safety of lithium-ion batteries during operation.

ce:section.ce:para: With the extensive application of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles [1–4] and renewable energy storage systems [5], their safety issues have garnered increasing attention [6]. Lithium-ion batteries are typically assembled into modules, where individual cells are subjected to fixed mechanical constraints. During the charging process, lithium-ion batteries undergo significant volume expansion, which generates substantial pressure within the battery pack. This pressure can adversely affect battery performance and even compromise safety. The volume expansion during normal charging can be categorized into lithiation-induced expansion and thermal expansion. Lithiation-induced expansion arises from crystalline volume changes in cathode and anode materials during lithiation (de-lithiation) [7,8]. Thermal expansion results from temperature increases caused by heat generation during charging and is closely related to the thermal expansion coefficients of electrode materials.

Recent studies have revealed that external pressure applied to batteries significantly influences their impedance characteristics and capacity decay rate, prompting increased research focus on the mechanical performance of lithium-ion batteries. Under mechanical constraints, the stress generated by battery expansion has been identified as a primary factor contributing to capacity degradation and safety concerns. Studies on single-cell pressure effects demonstrate that moderate pressure levels can reduce battery impedance [9] and decelerate capacity fading rates [10,11], while excessive pressure induces deformation or damage to battery components [12,13], thereby accelerating performance deterioration and triggering safety risks [14,15]. To effectively control the pressure inside battery systems, it is crucial to accurately quantify the swelling force generated by the battery's expansion behavior.

Based on the observed expansion behavior in lithium-ion batteries, researchers have established modeling frameworks to quantitatively characterize swelling force. At the macroscopic scale, swelling force can be quantified using equivalent mechanical models and phenomenological approaches. Jiang et al. [16] proposed an equivalent mechanical model that comprehensively accounts for stack pressure within battery assemblies. This model composed of simplified mechanical components, achieves both simplicity and accuracy, making it suitable for real-time applications. Choi et al. [17] developed a phenomenological model to define the mechanical behavior at the cell level. By integrating a one-dimensional equivalent mechanical model with the phenomenological framework and considering geometric and model uncertainties at the module level, they estimated swelling force. Yang et al. [18] established a hierarchical three-dimensional electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupled model to simulate the battery's expansion displacement during discharge under free expansion conditions. The obtained expansion displacement curve was then incorporated into the phenomenological model to estimate the magnitude of swelling force. Kwak et al. [19] employed a phenomenological force model to predict the evolution of force during charge and discharge. The equivalent stiffness was estimated separately based on the state of charge to account for the intrinsic characteristics of phase transitions and different degradation behaviors.

The charge-discharge process of lithium-ion batteries is inherently a complex multiphysics phenomenon involving dynamic coupling of electrochemical, thermodynamic, mechanical, and mass transfer processes. Traditional single-domain mechanical models often fail to accurately capture battery behavior during cycling, primarily because the generation and evolution of swelling force fundamentally stem from multiphysics coupling effects. Consequently, multiphysics coupled simulation has become an essential approach to overcome the limitations of single-discipline modeling. For battery responses under dynamic loading, electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupled models have been proposed, effectively describing mechanical responses under abuse conditions and thermal runaway evolution triggered by internal short circuits [20–22]. In the field of battery expansion mechanics, researchers have conducted simulation-based characterizations across particle, electrode, and cell scales. Liao et al. [23] proposed a three-dimensional high-fidelity model based on a multi-particle finite element method, which reconstructs the stacking structure of active particles to enable multi-scale mechanical analysis of active materials. Xiong et al. [24] proposed a thermo-electro-mechanical model with a piecewise nonlinear volume strain model as its core. The equivalent circuit model and equivalent thermal model provide the state of charge and battery temperature as inputs to the equivalent mechanical model, which then outputs the mechanical swelling force. Cai et al. [25] proposed a battery swelling force estimation method that integrates an electromechanical coupling model with machine learning. This method incorporates a reduced-order impedance model and a data-driven mechanical model, coupling the electrochemical state and mechanical state through the state of charge and mechanical pressure. Li et al. [26,27] determined the thermal expansion coefficient and equivalent stiffness of the battery through experiments and established an electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupling model. Using this model, they calculated the battery's overpotential to assess the impact of lithium plating on swelling force. Lou et al. [28] coupled stress fields at particle and representative volume element scales to study external load effects, demonstrating that mechanical loading significantly influences electrochemical behavior. Sauerteig et al. [29] developed an electrochemical-mechanical model that incorporates the effects of intercalation-induced electrode expansion, stress induced by mechanical boundaries, compression of electrodes and separators, and ion transport within the electrolyte. Cao et al. [30] developed a multi-scale model incorporating C-rate effects on expansion behavior, revealing rate-dependent multi-scale expansion mechanisms in graphite anode-based batteries. Bai et al. [31] investigated the variation of battery expansion displacement under different constant current charge-discharge rates and established an electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupling model for battery volume expansion. This model, which accounts for the effects of current rate and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) pore size, accurately simulates the battery expansion displacement at various rates. Building upon the electrochemical model, an expansion model was developed that incorporates SEI growth reactions and a physical description of electrode thickness expansion. By coupling the interaction between cell swelling and SEI reaction kinetics, this model enables the evaluation of both irreversible and total thickness expansion throughout battery operation [32,33]. Mei et al. [34] experimentally validated a 3D thermo-mechanical coupled model to characterize thermal expansion behavior in prismatic NMC lithium-ion batteries. Wang et al. [35] proposed a multiscale modeling method for electrochemical-mechanical coupling in lithium-ion batteries, enabling cross-scale modeling from battery components to cells. Leveraging this model, a systematic analysis was conducted to characterize the full-field strain and stress distributions of battery units throughout the charging process.

Current research primarily focuses on diffusion-induced stress arising from particle-level lithium intercalation at the microscale and mechanical responses under abuse conditions at the macroscale, while the quantitative characterization of swelling force during normal battery operation remains insufficiently explored. Existing multiphysics models are mostly confined to the particle and electrode scales, and the limited studies conducted at the cell scale fail to accurately reflect the practical layered stacking configurations of electrodes. Notably, few models achieve comprehensive electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupling, thereby restricting an in-depth understanding of the underlying mechanisms of battery swelling. To address these gaps, this study first establishes a synchronized experimental platform enabling concurrent measurement of swelling force, voltage, and temperature to capture macroscopic behavioral features of lithium-ion batteries. Furthermore, by considering the actual layered structure of cells, a detailed three-dimensional electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupled model is developed. In simulating swelling force, the model simultaneously incorporates both lithiation-induced and thermal expansion effects. Finally, experimental validation demonstrates strong consistency between the simulated and measured results of voltage, temperature, and swelling force, thereby confirming the accuracy and reliability of the proposed model. The main contributions of this paper are as follows.
1)
A multiphysics coupling framework encompassing the electrochemical, thermal, and swelling mechanical domains in lithium-ion batteries was established, enabling accurate simulation of these three physical fields. This work provides a multiphysics theoretical framework for the design of battery structural reliability and offers scientific insights for the optimization of cell structure and module assembly.
2)
Considering the actual layered structure of batteries, this study innovatively developed a highly detailed three-dimensional model. This model establishes the correlation between the component scale and the cell scale of the battery, which not only can reflect the stress distribution and deformation characteristics of each layer, but also can quantitatively characterize the dynamic evolution of macroscopic swelling force.
3)
The swelling force of batteries during charging was quantified, and a differential analysis method was employed to confirm a significant correlation between electrochemical performance and swelling force behavior. Furthermore, model-based investigation revealed the underlying mechanism responsible for the differential growth rates of swelling force across distinct stages.

The remaining chapters of this study are organized as follows: Section 2 describes the selected battery information, the constructed experimental platform, and the corresponding experimental procedure settings. Section 3 introduces the detailed process of model development, including the theoretical equations required for each physical field, relevant parameters, and associated model configurations. Section 4 validates the established model and subsequently analyzes the potential distribution, temperature distribution, and component stress-strain conditions.

In this study, fresh batteries were first subjected to three standard charge-discharge cycles to fully activate them. Subsequently, charging experiments were conducted at 25 °C under different preload forces and varying C-rates, during which voltage, temperature, and swelling force responses were measured. To ensure consistent initial charging conditions for each experiment and eliminate discrepancies in the initial swelling force between the cathode and anode electrodes, a 0.05C discharge current was applied after each charging cycle until reaching the lower cutoff voltage. The battery was then placed in the fixture and subjected to a preload force. Due to stress relaxation phenomena, the surface pressure on the battery gradually decreases after tightening the nuts. One key reason for this is that the binders in the cathode and anode electrode active materials, as well as the separator material, are inherently polymeric. These materials exhibit significant stress relaxation behavior under external loads, while the active materials demonstrate plastic characteristics. Together, these factors lead to a gradual reduction in the mechanical pressure acting on the battery. Another contributing factor is the flow of the electrolyte inside the battery. Under external pressure, the liquid electrolyte redistributes itself to accommodate potential deformations [36].

To ensure uniform preload force applied by the four preload nuts to the fixed steel plate, identical torque was applied to each nut using a torque wrench during the experiment. Therefore, the applied preload was intentionally set slightly higher than the target value, allowing it to stabilize at the predetermined level during subsequent resting periods. Given the inherent weight of the steel plates used to constrain the battery, the minimum preload force was set to 50 N, followed by 250 N. Charging C-rates were configured at 1C and 2C. To prevent heat dissipation through contact with the steel plates, thermal insulation boards were placed on both the top and bottom surfaces of the battery. Due to space constraints, the two temperature sensors were positioned at the midpoint of the battery's two side surfaces. Alignment of experimental datasets was performed with absolute time as reference. The complete experimental design is summarized in Table 2 .

The model in this study was developed using COMSOL Multiphysics, incorporating three physics interfaces: lithium-ion battery, solid mechanics, and heat transfer in solids. The overall coupled framework is illustrated in Fig. 2 . We developed a bidirectional coupling between the electrochemical model and the thermal model. Experimental results indicated minimal variation in the charge voltage profiles under the implemented preload, suggesting that a one-way coupling between the electrochemical and mechanical models is adequate. Here, the electrochemical model calculates the state of charge of the electrodes, which is then input into the mechanical model to compute lithiation-induced swelling due to lithium-ion intercalation. Similarly, the thermal model and mechanical model are one-way coupled, with the thermal model computing the temperature distribution of internal battery components and feeding it into the mechanical model to determine thermal expansion caused by temperature rise. The battery cell can be considered as multiple parallel-connected repeating battery units. Each unit consists of two layers of cathode active material, two layers of anode active material, two layers of separator, one layer of cathode current collector, and two layers of anode current collector. In the actual battery structure, the cathode and anode current collectors feature extended regions. Multiple layers of these extended regions are interconnected to enable current inflow/outflow through repeating units. Therefore, the battery cell can be considered as multiple pairs of parallel-connected repeating units. Given the nearly identical states across these repeating units, a single representative unit was selected for electrochemical modeling to reduce computational costs, while maintaining the actual structural configuration. The thermal and mechanical models were constructed based on the real stacked architecture of the battery components, with corresponding physics settings applied.

In this study, the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) electrochemical model developed by Newman and Doyle et al. will be adopted. This model is based on porous electrode theory and concentrated solution theory, describing the following key processes: solid-phase and liquid-phase diffusion of lithium ions, electrochemical reactions at the solid-liquid interface, ohm's law for charge transport in both phases, and charge conservation during reactions. In the present model, the current collectors (both anode and cathode) are explicitly considered and defined as “electrodes” in the physics settings. The electrode current is applied at the top of the cathode electrode current collector, while the bottom of the anode electrode current collector is grounded to set its electrical potential to zero. The open-circuit potentials (OCP) of the anode and cathode [37] are illustrated in Fig. 3 (a).

The transport of lithium ions in the electrolyte occurs solely through diffusion and migration, with the transport process described by Fick's second law.

represents volume fraction of the liquid phase;

D e
represents diffusion coefficient in the liquid phase; x represents direction along the electrode thickness;
a
represents specific surface area of active particles per unit volume;
t + 0
represents transference number of lithium ions in the liquid phase;
j r
represents lithium-ion flux density.

The diffusion of lithium ions within the anode and cathode active materials is also described by Fick's second law. The active particles in both electrodes are assumed to be spherical particles with equal radii, and the diffusion equation is expressed as follows:

is the solid-phase diffusion coefficient.

The variation of the liquid-phase potential in lithium-ion batteries is described by Ohm's law:

In lithium-ion batteries, the sum of solid-phase current density and liquid-phase current density at any position equals the total charge/discharge current density, expressed as:

represents the applied current density.

The Butler-Volmer equation is widely used to describe the electrochemical reaction kinetics at the solid-liquid interface (electrode-electrolyte interface) in lithium-ion batteries, expressed as:

Thermal models consist of heat generation models and heat transfer models. The battery's heat generation rate

The governing equation is the energy conservation equation, expressed as:

where

represents the constant-pressure heat capacity;

is the anisotropic thermal conductivity tensor. The entropic heat coefficients of anode and cathode materials [38] are shown in Fig. 3(b).

In our experiments, the battery was not in direct contact with the steel clamping plates but were separated by thermal insulation boards, which effectively suppressed heat conduction. Under these conditions, the heat transfer between the battery and the steel plates could be reasonably neglected in the model. The heat generated within the battery is transferred to its surface through internal thermal conduction. The temperature calculated by the thermal model is used to calibrate temperature-sensitive parameters in the electrochemical model adjusted parameters are listed in Table 3 .

In this model, both lithiation-induced swelling and thermal expansion are considered. The active particles in the electrode are assumed to consist solely of isotropic, linearly elastic spheres [18,36]. Within the scope of this study-where the swelling force remains relatively moderate-the linear elastic spherical approximation is sufficient to capture the dominant electrochemical-mechanical behavior. Based on this assumption, an electrochemically-mechanically coupled model is developed, and the stress-strain constitutive relationship is expressed as shown in the following equation:
(13)
ε
i j
=
1 E
1 + ν
σ
i j
− ν
σ
k k
δ
i j
+
fun
SOC
3
δ
i j
+ α Δ Τ
δ
i j
where
ε
i j
is the strain tensor;
σ
i j
is the stress tensor; α is the thermal expansion coefficient;
E
is Young's modulus;
ν
is Poisson's ratio;
δ
i j
is the Kronecker delta function; fun
SOC
represents the volumetric change rate of the active materials (anode/cathode) as a function of their respective state of charge [39–41], as illustrated in Figs. 3(c) and (d).

In the boundary conditions of solid mechanics, roller support constraints are applied to both the upper and lower surfaces of the battery, with all other surfaces set to free conditions. Since the tabs do not experience lithium intercalation-induced expansion, they are excluded from the simulation. This model defines all battery components as linear elastic materials. For the active materials of the cathode and anode electrodes, intercalation strain is specified by referencing the electrode state of charge calculated from the electrochemical model to compute the volumetric change rate of the electrodes. Under boundary constraints, the swelling force caused by lithium intercalation is simulated. Regarding thermal expansion, thermal strain is introduced at the nodes of linear elastic materials based on the temperature distribution calculated by the thermal model. The swelling force due to thermal effects is then computed under constrained boundary conditions. To simulate the initial preload force magnitude, external stress is applied at the nodes of linear elastic materials. During the simulation, the three physical fields (electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical) are computed simultaneously, with mutual coupling between them. The model takes into account the aluminum laminate film, with its parameters listed in Table 4 . All parameters required for the three physical fields are listed in Table 5 . The simulation parameters adopted are based on data from the literature and standard data from the COMSOL material library.

Upon completion of the physical field setup, grid generation was performed for each geometric model. Given that the tab portion does not exhibit lithium intercalation-induced swelling behavior, the geometric model of the mechanical model was constructed by removing the tab structure from the thermal model. Meanwhile, the thermal model already incorporates multiple battery units; to ensure consistency across multi-physics simulations, the grid generation strategies for both the electrochemical model and the mechanical model were directly adopted from the thermal model and replicated accordingly.

A structured grid generation method was employed for the thermal model: regular free quadrilateral grids were first generated on the source surface, followed by a sweeping operation to propagate the grid through the domain between the source and target surfaces. The final generated grid comprises 14,928 hexahedral elements. Prior to analyzing the simulation results, grid independence verification was performed. Specifically, the original grid was refined and coarsened, respectively. The verification was performed using the simulation of voltage, swelling force, and temperature during the 2C charging process under a 250 N preload as the case study, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The results demonstrate that the simulation outcomes corresponding to the coarsened grid exhibit slight deviations, whereas those obtained with the refined grid are nearly identical to the results from the original grid. Therefore, to balance computational precision and efficiency, the original grid was adopted for all subsequent simulations, as it not only meets the requirements for calculation accuracy but also effectively reduces computational costs.

The variations in swelling force, voltage, and temperature of the battery during the charging process are shown in Fig. 5 . Regarding the swelling force characteristics: during the charging process, the anode electrode active material undergoes lithium intercalation and expansion, while the cathode electrode active material contracts due to lithium deintercalation. Since the expansion degree of the anode electrode active material is greater than the contraction degree of the cathode electrode active material, the overall swelling force of the battery increases. It is observed that the swelling force growth curve exhibits a turning point at approximately 1,600 s during 1C charging and around 800 s during 2C charging. The reasons for this will be explored and analyzed in Section 4.4. During constant-current charging at the same rate, the variation in swelling force exhibits slight differences under different preload conditions. Under higher preload forces, the amplitude of swelling force growth during the final charging stage is greater than that under lower preload conditions. This phenomenon primarily occurs because higher preload forces restrict physical expansion space, enhance the elastic response of materials, and optimize interfacial contact, thereby more directly converting volumetric changes during charging into increased internal pressure.

Comparing swelling force variations under the same preload but different charging rates reveals that higher rates result in greater post-charging swelling force than lower rates. This difference is mainly attributed to temperature effects, as higher charging rates induce more significant temperature rises, amplifying thermal expansion. Notably, voltage curves show almost no variation across different preload levels, indicating minimal influence of preload on voltage. Thus, the unidirectional coupling between the electrochemical model and solid mechanics model in this study is validated as reasonable. Regarding thermal characteristics, temperature sensors placed on both sides of the battery recorded minor discrepancies (≤0.1 °C). Due to the small battery capacity selected, the maximum temperature rise during 2C-rate charging was approximately 1.5 °C. Generally, the battery temperature slightly decreased during the initial charging phase, then gradually increased. Toward the end of charging, the heat generation rate declined, leading to a gradual temperature reduction.

Differential analysis can sensitively capture subtle changes in electromechanical properties, providing auxiliary support for establishing the correlation between swelling force and electrical performance. The curve of swelling force change rate with respect to charging capacity during the charging process exhibits one peak and one trough, as shown in Fig. 6 (b), roughly dividing the swelling force growth trend into three stages: “rapid - slow - rapid again”. Meanwhile, the peak of this curve corresponds to the extreme point of the dV/dQ. In addition, both the dF/dV and dQ/dV curves show two characteristic peaks with a slight offset in peak positions-the dQ/dV peaks lag behind the dF/dV peaks. This phenomenon can be explained by the viscoelastic effect, meaning that the stress induced by lithium intercalation requires a certain time to accumulate and manifest as macroscopic strain [45]. Voltage-driven lithium-ion migration initially triggers immediate stress; however, the delayed stress relaxation caused by viscoelasticity impedes the instant and uniform diffusion of lithium ions. Consequently, the peak of the dQ/dV curve is only achieved after the stress is released through viscous deformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation and analysis of the electrochemical model
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 7 (a) and (b), the simulated voltage results under varying preload forces are compared with experimental measurements. Under high current rates, electrode polarization effects become significantly more pronounced. Notably, the refined electrochemical model developed in this study accurately captures this polarization phenomenon, demonstrating excellent agreement between simulated and experimental voltage curves. Meanwhile, to further verify the reliability of the established model, supplementary validation was conducted at an ambient temperature of 40 °C, with additional validation performed under dynamic operating conditions. The relevant results are presented in Appendix Figs. S1, S6 and S7. Overall, the model exhibits high voltage prediction accuracy and effectively reflects the operational voltage characteristics of the battery.

The potential distribution on the aluminum collector throughout the charging process is depicted in Figs. 7(c)–(f). In this model configuration, the negative direction of the X-axis corresponds to the position of the cathode tab; in the actual structure, the aluminum collector has an extended region, which is connected to the tab. Simulation results indicate that the regions close to the cathode tab maintain a slightly higher potential than those far from the tab, and the potential distribution of the aluminum collector during high-rate charging is more non-uniform compared to that during low-rate charging. This phenomenon primarily stems from ohmic voltage drop along the current collector surface: as charging current flows from the tab and spreads across the collector, cumulative path resistance causes progressive potential reduction in distal regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation and analysis of the thermal model
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 8 (a) and (b), the comparative results between temperature simulation and experimental measurements during the entire charging process, the maximum temperature rise increases significantly under high C-rate conditions. The heat generation rate decreases toward the end of charging, leading to temperature reduction. Since two temperature sensors were physically installed at the center of the battery's side surfaces, corresponding probes were placed at identical locations in the model. The average probe temperature was adopted as the simulated value. The dual probes in the thermal model effectively captured the battery's temperature evolution trend. The evolution process of temperature distribution on the battery surface during the charging process is shown in Figs. 8(c)–(f). During the experiment, due to heat exchange with the ambient air, the aluminum plastic film on the periphery of the battery had the lowest temperature. Conversely, the large surface of the battery in contact with the heat insulation board had the highest temperature. In addition, the battery surface showed a uniform temperature distribution. Meanwhile, to further verify the reliability of the established model, additional validation was conducted at an ambient temperature of 40 °C, with the relevant results presented in Appendix Fig. S2. In conclusion, this model has high temperature simulation accuracy and can reliably reflect the thermal characteristics under operating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation and analysis of the mechanical model
ce:section.ce:para: In the model, transient calculations were performed to determine the pressure distribution on the battery surface at each time step. The total swelling force was obtained by integrating over the battery's large surface area. The simulation results of battery swelling force during charging are compared with experimental measurements in Figs. 9 (a) and (b), showing good agreement between the simulated curve and experimental data.

Table 6 summarizes the contributions of lithiation-induced expansion and thermal expansion to the total swelling force at the end of charging. The results indicate that at 1C rate, the contribution of thermal expansion to the swelling force is almost negligible compared with that of lithiation-induced expansion. However, at 2C rate, the temperature rise of the battery increases significantly, and the proportion of thermal expansion force in the total swelling force also enhances accordingly.

The surface pressure distribution on the battery surface during the charging process is shown in Figs. 9(c)–(f). In the early and middle stages of charging, the maximum pressure on the battery surface is concentrated in the central area of the battery surface. At the end of charging, the simulation results under different preload forces show opposite outcomes. At the end of charging: under lower preload forces, the maximum surface pressure occurs at the battery periphery; while under higher preload forces, it appears at the center of the battery surface. This is primarily due to the Poisson effect causing in-plane expansion when the battery is compressed in the thickness direction. At the battery-fixture interface, microscopic irregularities or initial gaps may exist. Small preload forces cannot fully eliminate these gaps, causing pressure to transfer more easily through edge paths. In contrast, large preload forces ensure complete contact between the battery and fixture, eliminating initial gaps. Pressure is then uniformly distributed across the entire contact surface, with stress concentrations at the edges being alleviated by material yielding or creep, resulting in a more balanced pressure distribution.

The stress and strain distributions of battery components are shown in Fig. 10 . Simultaneously perform local magnification and plotting of the stress-strain results for a pair of repeating units inside the battery. The cathode active material exhibits the highest stress level, followed by the anode active material. Regarding strain conditions, the anode active material shows cathode strain (indicating expansion), while the cathode active material displays anode strain (indicating contraction), with the anode active material demonstrating greater strain magnitude. Due to the higher Young's modulus of the cathode active material compared to the anode, the cathode develops larger stresses despite its smaller strain. The strain of the anode and cathode electrode current collectors is the smallest. This is because current collectors typically use metal materials, which possess high ductility, high strength, and a large elastic modulus, making them less prone to significant deformation when subjected to a certain level of stress.

The increase in swelling force during charging is primarily caused by the swelling of anode active materials. The lattice parameters of graphite, such as interlayer spacing, change with phase transitions, while the expansion of anode active particles is induced by lithium intercalation. Therefore, the generation of swelling force is essentially related to the lithium concentration in particles, i.e., the solid-phase lithium ion concentration in active particles. The nonlinear variation of solid-phase lithium ion concentration leads to the nonlinear increase in swelling force during charging.

Taking the condition of 250 N preload as an example, three typical positions in the anode active material were selected to observe the lithium intercalation concentration of particles: Point P1 is the center point of the interface between the anode active material and the anode current collector, P2 is the center point of the anode active material, and P3 is the center point of the interface between the anode active material and the separator. The evolution patterns of lithium intercalation concentration and lithiation level at the three positions over time are shown in Figs. 11 (a) and (b). It can be found that the lithium intercalation concentrations at the three positions are comparable in the early stage of charging. Subsequently, the lithium intercalation rate of particles at position P3 increases, reaching the maximum achievable lithium intercalation concentration of the anode first, followed by position P2, and finally position P1.

This is mainly because the region far from the cathode requires lithium ions to traverse longer electrolyte paths and electrode thicknesses, resulting in increased transport resistance and decreased diffusion rate. Meanwhile, the lithium ions released by the cathode exhibit a concentration gradient in the electrolyte: the concentration is higher and the driving force is stronger in the region close to the cathode, while the concentration gradually decreases and the lithium intercalation driving force weakens in the region far from the cathode. It can be found that even at the end of charging, the lithium-ion concentration in the region close to the anode current collector remains at a low level. A higher charging rate leads to greater inhomogeneity in the distribution of lithium intercalation concentration.

The moment corresponding to the turning point of the swelling force coincides with the time when the lithium intercalation rate at position P3 begins to increase significantly compared to the other two positions. This moment corresponds to a lithiation level of approximately 0.33, which falls within the 2 L phase, as shown in Table 7 . By the time of the swelling force turning point, the layered graphite compound in the anode has already completed the phase transition from 2H to 2 L, and the phase transitions occurring at different locations of the anode active material are consistent. In the empty state of the battery, the graphite anode is in the 2H phase. As charging progresses, phase transitions gradually occur. During the 1 L→4 L→3 L phase transition process, the thickness of the graphite anode increases rapidly, leading to a rapid rise in swelling force. In the 3 L→2 L phase transition, the increase in graphite interlayer spacing is relatively small, and the swelling force growth rate reaches its maximum at this stage. As charging continues, the subsequent phase transitions result in smaller increases in the thickness of the graphite anode. The volume change rate curve of the anode active material also reflects this phenomenon. After the state of charge of the anode reaches 0.4, the growth rate of volume change slows down, ultimately causing the swelling force growth rate to decrease, manifesting as the swelling force turning phenomenon. During the 2 → 1 phase transition of the graphite anode, the thickness of the anode increases rapidly again. Simultaneously, the lithium intercalation rates at positions P2 and P1 also increase significantly, leading to another rapid growth stage in the swelling force. Notably, in the later stages of charging, the graphite anode may contain phases with varying degrees of lithiation.

Based on the established experimental platform for lithium-ion battery swelling force, this study systematically investigates the battery's swelling characteristics. The experimental results clearly identify and quantitatively characterize the swelling force induced by expansion effects. Furthermore, by integrating the observed patterns of voltage, temperature, and swelling force evolution, a coupled multiphysics model has been developed for simulation and quantitative analysis. The main conclusions are as follows.
(1)
Under two different preload forces and two charging rates, the overall variation in battery swelling force remained within 65 N. Additionally, a higher charging rate resulted in a slightly larger swelling force increase at the end of charging compared to a lower rate, primarily due to the more pronounced thermal expansion effect under fast charging. The swelling force growth magnitude under high preload exceeds that under low preload. This is mainly due to high preload restricting physical expansion space, enhancing material elastic response. These effects collectively allow the volume change during charging to be more directly converted.
(2)
This study fully considers the actual layered structural characteristics of batteries and proposes a method for constructing a high-precision three-dimensional electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupling model. By incorporating the volume change rates of cathode and anode active materials, the model establishes a correlation between the component scale and the cell scale, enabling quantitative characterization of the dynamic evolution process of macroscopic swelling force. It is verified that the simulation results of the established model for voltage, temperature, and swelling force all exhibit high accuracy and reliability.
(3)
Simulation results indicate that under lower preload conditions, the maximum surface pressure occurs at the battery's periphery, whereas under higher preload, the peak pressure shifts to the center of the battery surface. The stress in the cathode active material reaches its maximum at the end of charging, whereas its strain is minimal; the anode active material exhibits exactly the opposite behavior. This phenomenon is primarily ascribed to the difference in Young's modulus between the cathode and anode active materials.
(4)
The generation of swelling force is inherently associated with the solid-phase lithium ion concentration in active particles, and the nonlinear variation of the solid-phase lithium ion concentration leads to a nonlinear increase in swelling force during the charging process. The moment corresponding to the turning point of the swelling force coincides with the time when the lithium intercalation rate at position P3 starts to increase significantly relative to the other two positions. In the later stage of charging, phases with different lithiation degrees exist in the graphite anode.

This study focuses on the variation law of swelling force in lithium-ion batteries under different preloads and charging rates, while temporarily excluding the influence of side reactions such as lithium plating and gas generation on the swelling force. Future research will further expand to investigate the mechanism of the effect of low-temperature lithium plating on swelling force, as well as the evolution law of swelling force under dynamic working conditions, aiming to comprehensively improve the quantitative understanding of battery swelling forces. The model provides detailed simulation results of the internal stress distribution between battery layers; however, experimental validation of these key results is not presented, with the only experimental data being the measurement of overall swelling force. Developing methods for direct measurement or validation of internal stress remains a significant challenge for future research. In subsequent studies, we will prioritize computational resources to conduct a systematic sensitivity analysis of key parameters, thereby evaluating the reliability of the simulated internal stress results of the battery.

Quanqing Yu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Liubin Fan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Huanyong Deng: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Donglin Fang: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Can Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. ZR2025MS715), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52177210 and U24A20158), and the Project of Key Laboratory of Advanced Manufacturing Technology for Automobile Parts, Ministry of Education (Grant No. 2024KLMT02).
